---
title: "Bayes Regression - Example"
format: html
---

Algorithm for Union Falls Pond:

Questions:
- Line 53: how do you know what values are best?

```{r}
# step 0 - load in data and packages

library(tidyverse)

# step 1 - write out the vb function, grab from vb quarto

vb_function <- function(t, max_l, k, b) {
  max_l * (1 - exp(-k * (t - b)))}

# step 2 - compute prior for max_l

# remember prior is important because it specifies the possible values and the relative prior plausibility of each

# prior for max_l in my model should be 0 (log1) so its not a factor

compute_prior_log <- function(max_l, k, b) {
  dnorm(max_l, mean = 0, sd = 1000, log = TRUE)
  dnorm(k, mean = 0, sd = 1000, log = TRUE)
  dnorm(b, mean = 0, sd = 1000, log = TRUE)
}

#  fix k and b in my model, get from model - if you run it you find the values

k <-  0.3183 # k # used to be beta0
b <-  -0.8749 # b # used to be sigma1
sigma2 <- summary(unionfallspond_vb)$sigma^2 # treat the summary as the data set to pull out the sd for the function

compute_likelihood_log <- function(max_l, k, b) {

  dnorm(predict_unionfallspond$Length, mean = von_bert_function(clams_unionfallspond$growth_period, max_l, k, b),
        sd = sqrt(sigma2), log = TRUE) |>
    sum() # call function
  
  ## sum log likelihoods instead of multiplying like you would do with non-logged likelihoods
  ## can think back to the math-stat part of probability- the mean just differs depending on
  
}

compute_likelihood_log(max_l = 30, k = 0.1, b = 0)
compute_likelihood_log(max_l = 40, k = 0.1, b = 0)
compute_likelihood_log(max_l = 50, k = 0.1, b = 0)
## higher likelihoods are "better"


## combining prior and data/likelihood
## again, summing not multiplying because we add things on the log scale
compute_posterior_log <- function(max_l, k, b) {
  compute_prior_log(max_l, k, b) + compute_likelihood_log(max_l, k, b)
}

compute_posterior_log(max_l = 30, k = 0.1, b = 0)
compute_posterior_log(max_l = 40, k = 0.1, b = 0)
compute_posterior_log(max_l = 50, k = 0.1, b = 0)


## define a proposal/jumping distribution to be N(0, 0.1)


## set-up the chain
niter <- 10000

# set up three empty vectors, one for each parameter
max_l_store <- rep(NA, 10000) 
k_store <- rep(NA, 10000)
b_store <- rep(NA, 10000)

# define a starting value
max_l_store[1] <- 1.2 
k_store[1] <- k
b_store[1] <- b

for (i in 2:niter) {
  max_l_current <- max_l_store[i - 1]
  k_current <- k_store[i - 1]
  b_current <- b_store[i - 1]

  ## propose a new value with proposal/jumping distribution
  ## here, my proposal/jumping distribution is N(current_beta1, 0.1)
  ## this distribution must be symmetric around the current beta1 (or else would need adjustment)
  proposal_sd <- 0.01
  ## proposal_sd can't be too large or too small, as discussed in the reading.

  max_l_proposal <- rnorm(1, mean = max_l_current, sd = proposal_sd)
  k_proposal <- rnorm(1, mean = k_current, sd = proposal_sd)
  b_proposal <- rnorm(1, mean = b_current, sd = proposal_sd)

  ## compute acceptance probability - do this in machine learning i think
  alpha_l <- min(1, exp(compute_posterior_log(max_l_proposal) - compute_posterior_log(max_l_current)))
  
   alpha_k <- min(1, exp(compute_posterior_log(k_proposal) - compute_posterior_log(k_current)))
   
    alpha_b <- min(1, exp(compute_posterior_log(b_proposal) - compute_posterior_log(b_current)))

  ## maybe update beta1 with acceptance probability alpha
  max_l_store[i] <- sample(c(max_l_proposal, max_l_current), size = 1,
                           prob = c(alpha_l, 1 - alpha_l))
  k_store[i] <- sample(c(k_proposal, k_current), size = 1,
                           prob = c(alpha_k, 1 - alpha_k))
  b_store[i] <- sample(c(b_proposal, b_current), size = 1,
                           prob = c(alpha_b, 1 - alpha_b))
}

## plots
## filter removes "start-up"
plot_df <- tibble(iter = 1:niter, max_l = max_l_store, k = k_store, b = b_store) |>
  filter(iter > 2000)

## make sure that this seems "stable" (isn't trailing off in an increasing
## or decreasing direction)
ggplot(data = plot_df, aes(x = iter, y = max_l)) +
  geom_line()

plot_df |>
  summarise(mean_l = mean(max_l),
            mean_k = mean(k),
            mean_b = mean(b),
            sd = sd(max_l))
## check these with estimator for beta1 and its SE from lm: they should be close!

## shape of posterior
ggplot(data = plot_df, aes(x = max_l)) +
  geom_histogram(colour = "skyblue4", fill = "skyblue1", bins = 15)

```
